ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for sunny-side up egg preparation; FoodOn and FoodEx2 anchors are sufficient.

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical sunny-side up egg weighs approximately 46 g (one large egg without shell, pan-fried with minimal oil).

nutrient_source.title: Egg, whole, cooked, fried — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 173424)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry for whole fried egg used as primary nutrient reference. HPB Singapore input values for energy (55 kcal/100g), protein (10.7 g/100g), and carbohydrate (2.36 g/100g) are noted but diverge from USDA SR Legacy (196 kcal, 13.6 g protein, 0.83 g carbohydrate per 100g). The HPB input values appear inconsistent (energy 55 kcal is implausibly low for a fried egg with fat present, and fat reported as 0.0 g is contradicted by the frying method). USDA SR Legacy values are adopted as the authoritative source. Fat_g is confirmed non-zero per USDA SR Legacy (14.8 g/100g for fried egg). The HPB fat value of 0.0 g is rejected as unsupported for a pan-fried egg preparation.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index not applicable — sunny-side up egg contains negligible carbohydrates (0.83 g/100g)
gi_source.source_match_notes: Sunny-side up egg is a high-protein, high-fat food with negligible carbohydrate content (0.83 g/100g). GI testing requires a minimum carbohydrate load of 25–50 g; this food cannot produce a measurable GI response. The University of Sydney GI Database was consulted and confirms no GI value is published for fried egg. GI is set to -1 (data not available / not applicable) and gi_category to not_applicable per Veritas protocol for negligible-carbohydrate foods. Glycemic load per serving is effectively 0.0.

hypertension.guidance: A single sunny-side up egg (46 g) contributes approximately 95 mg sodium, which is acceptable within a low-sodium diet (target <1500–2000 mg/day), but patients with hypertension should avoid adding salt during cooking and limit accompanying high-sodium condiments such as soy sauce or sambal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Negligible carbohydrate (0.83 g/100g); high protein (13.6 g/100g) supports satiety and glycaemic stability
type2_diabetes.guidance: Sunny-side up egg has negligible carbohydrate content and a high protein-to-energy ratio, making it an excellent low-glycaemic food for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; use minimal cooking oil and avoid pairing with high-GI staples such as white rice or white bread to maintain overall meal glycaemic control.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Dietary cholesterol 401 mg/100g; saturated fat 4.0 g/100g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit egg consumption to no more than 3–4 eggs per week in line with current evidence-based guidelines, use heart-healthy oils (e.g., olive oil or canola oil) for frying, and monitor total dietary cholesterol and saturated fat intake from all sources across the day.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High dietary cholesterol (401 mg/100g) relevant for RA patients on corticosteroids with elevated cardiovascular risk; absence of omega-3 fatty acids or strong anti-inflammatory micronutrients
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Eggs provide vitamin D, vitamin E, and selenium which support anti-inflammatory pathways relevant to RA; however, the absence of omega-3 fatty acids and the high cholesterol content mean eggs should be limited to one per day for RA patients on corticosteroids, and frying should use extra-virgin olive oil to add MUFA benefit.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sunny-side up egg (Indonesian: Telur Ceplok) is a pan-fried egg preparation in which the egg is cracked into a lightly oiled pan and cooked until the white is fully set while the yolk remains runny or semi-runny, with the egg never flipped. It is a staple accompaniment in Indonesian and broader Southeast Asian cuisine, commonly served alongside instant noodles such as Indomie, steamed rice, or as part of a nasi campur plate. The texture contrasts a firm, slightly crisp-edged white with a rich, creamy yolk. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides approximately 196 kcal, 13.6 g protein, 14.8 g fat (of which 4.0 g saturated), and only 0.83 g carbohydrate, making it a high-protein, low-carbohydrate food. It is a good source of vitamin B12 (1.11 mcg), vitamin D (2.0 mcg), selenium (27.5 mcg), choline, and vitamin A (149 mcg RAE). Clinically, it is suitable for Type 2 Diabetes management due to negligible carbohydrate content, but dietary cholesterol (401 mg/100g) warrants moderation for cardiovascular disease and rheumatoid arthritis patients on corticosteroids. It is naturally gluten-free and halal.
common_user_queries: What is a sunny-side up egg?
How many calories in a fried egg?
Is sunny-side up egg good for diabetes?
Can I eat fried egg if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 173424), an approved primary source with an exact match for whole fried egg. HPB input values were reviewed and the fat value of 0.0 g was rejected as implausible for a pan-fried preparation; USDA SR Legacy values are adopted. GI is not applicable due to negligible carbohydrate content. Indonesian local name confirmed from standard culinary references.
notes: 1. The input HPB record reported fat_g as 0.0 g/100g for a pan-fried egg, which is nutritionally implausible. USDA SR Legacy reports 14.8 g fat/100g for fried egg. The HPB fat value is rejected and USDA SR Legacy is adopted as the authoritative source. fat_g is confirmed non-zero; no zero-macro justification applies. 2. The input HPB energy value of 55 kcal/100g is inconsistent with the macronutrient profile of a fried egg and is replaced by the USDA SR Legacy value of 196 kcal/100g. 3. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 g: eggs contain no dietary fibre — naturally zero. 4. trans_fat_g is 0.0 g: confirmed negligible/trace per USDA SR Legacy for pan-fried egg with vegetable oil — confirmed zero. 5. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 g: eggs contain no vitamin C — naturally zero. 6. monounsaturated_fat_g (5.7 g), polyunsaturated_fat_g (2.0 g), potassium_mg (152 mg), calcium_mg (62 mg), iron_mg (1.89 mg), cholesterol_mg (401 mg), and all micronutrient values sourced from USDA FDC SR Legacy FDC ID 173424. 7. glycemic_index is set to -1 (numeric sentinel for not available / not applicable): sunny-side up egg contains only 0.83 g carbohydrate per 100g, which is below the threshold required for GI testing. The University of Sydney GI Database was consulted and confirms no GI value is published for fried egg. gi_category is set to not_applicable. glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0. 8. The input health_suitability section referenced protein at 13.6 g/100g and carbohydrate at 0.6 g/100g, which aligns with USDA SR Legacy values rather than the HPB per_100g values in the same record, suggesting the health notes were written against a different data source. USDA SR Legacy values are used throughout this output for consistency.

q1_food_identity.notes: food_name is 'Sunny-Side Up Egg', a precise canonical English name. Indonesian local name 'Telur Ceplok' is present satisfying G6 multilingual requirement for Indonesian cuisine. Input alias 'Sunny Egg' preserved.

q2_core_macro_completeness_and_source_in.notes: energy_kcal (196.0), protein_g (13.6), fat_g (14.8), carbohydrate_g (0.83) all present and sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 173424. HPB input fat value of 0.0 rejected as implausible and documented in notes.

q3_secondary_nutrient_completeness.notes: dietary_fibre_g (0.0 — naturally zero, eggs contain no fibre), sodium_mg (207.0), saturated_fat_g (4.0), sugar_g (0.56) all present as numeric values sourced from USDA SR Legacy.

q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: glycemic_index set to -1 (numeric sentinel — GI not applicable for negligible-carbohydrate food; University of Sydney GI Database consulted and confirms no published GI for fried egg). gi_category set to 'not_applicable'. glycemic_load_per_serving set to 0.0. gi_source fully documented. This satisfies the q4 requirement that glycemic_index be numeric while correctly representing the not-applicable status for a food with 0.83 g carbohydrate/100g.

q5_ontological_alignment.notes: FoodOn URI http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/FOODON_03302051 (fried egg food product) and FoodEx2 code A00LN (Hen's egg, fried) are defensible anchors. SNOMED CT null with gap note documented.

q6_llm_ready_semantics.notes: natural_language_description is 1,147 characters, covers food identity, preparation method, texture, cultural context (Indonesian cuisine, Indomie pairing), clinical relevance (diabetes, CVD, RA), and key nutrients.

q7_health_context.notes: All three required conditions present: Hypertension (moderate, sodium/cholesterol), Type 2 Diabetes (low, negligible carbohydrate/high protein), Cardiovascular Disease (moderate, dietary cholesterol/saturated fat). Each has concern_level, key_factor, and specific clinical guidance sentence. human_condition_name 'Type 2 Diabetes' used correctly.
